facts.fact: Raft consensus algorithm was published by Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout at USENIX ATC 2014 as 'In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm', explicitly designed to be more understandable than Paxos
User study with 43 students showed 33 correctly answered more questions about Raft than Paxos after learning both algorithms, demonstrating Raft's superior understandability

The etcd Raft library (go.etcd.io/etcd/raft) is the most widely used Raft implementation in production, powering etcd, Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Cloud Foundry, CockroachDB, TiDB, Hyperledger, and Calico as of 2016
CockroachDB partitions data into ranges, each with its own Raft consensus group, meaning each node participates in hundreds of thousands of consensus groups (unlike etcd's single monolithic group)
Lamport timestamps use a simple counter that increments with every local event; on receiving a message, a process updates its counter to max(local_counter, received_counter) + 1
Vector clocks represent timestamps as an array with one integer per process (O(N) space complexity), while Lamport timestamps use a single integer (O(1) space)
Raft specifies election timeout should satisfy: broadcastTime ≪ electionTimeout ≪ MTBF, where broadcastTime is the average RTT and MTBF is mean time between failures
etcd default heartbeat interval is 100 milliseconds; default election timeout is 1000 milliseconds (10x multiplier recommended minimum)
A cluster accumulating 100 log entries per second produces 8,640,000 entries daily and 3,153,600,000 entries annually (~315 GB at 100 bytes/entry)
Raft requires 2f+1 nodes to tolerate f crash failures; Byzantine Fault Tolerance requires 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f byzantine failures
Idempotent consumer pattern requires recording processed message IDs in a PROCESSED_MESSAGES table keyed by unique messageId; duplicate detection prevents repeated execution
Kafka achieves exactly-once semantics using idempotent producers with Producer IDs (PIDs) and sequence numbers per partition, transactions, and transactional consumers
Azure Storage uses Paxos for managing consistency across distributed storage systems, ensuring write operations are reliably propagated to all replicas
NTP achieves ~100-500 microseconds on LANs but only 10-100ms on public internet; PTP (IEEE 1588) achieves sub-microsecond accuracy via hardware timestamping
Split-brain in Raft occurs when a network partition divides the cluster; nodes in the minority partition cannot elect a leader or accept writes
Write-ahead logging (WAL) appends changes before applying to main data store; Raft log entries transition through states: Appended → Replicated (majority) → Committed
Quorum rule: for N nodes, quorum is (N/2)+1 members; 3-node cluster tolerates 1 failure, 5-node cluster tolerates 2 failures; formula is 2f+1 nodes for f failures
Paxos implementation requires thousands of lines of C++ code despite concise pseudocode; Raft's separation of concerns (leader election, log replication, safety) reduces implementation complexity

The Eight Fallacies of Distributed Computing originated at Sun Microsystems: L. Peter Deutsch created seven in 1994, Bill Joy and Dave Lyon identified four earlier, James Gosling added the eighth around 1997
Dynatune (2024) extends Raft by dynamically tuning election timeout and heartbeat intervals based on real-time RTT and packet loss measurements
BALLAST (2024) uses bandit-assisted learning to dynamically tune Raft election timeouts, reducing false-positive leader elections while maintaining responsiveness

examples.example: Kubernetes stores all cluster state in etcd (ConfigMaps, Deployments, Pods, Services) using Raft consensus; etcd failures cause complete cluster unavailability until recovered
CockroachDB scales Raft by assigning each data range its own consensus group; a single node may participate in 500,000+ groups simultaneously
Kafka uses idempotent producers with sequence numbers to detect retries within a producer session; transactional writes ensure exactly-once at the consumer boundary
Google Spanner uses Paxos for inter-datacenter replication across 5+ regions; provides strong consistency with readable replicas at 10ms+ latency
HashiCorp Consul uses Raft for distributed state; split-brain protection requires >2 nodes; single-node leader failure triggers 500ms-1000ms election timeout
Amazon DynamoDB uses eventual consistency for global tables; write to primary region, async propagate to replicas; read-your-write guaranteed only at primary
Netflix engineering deploys Raft-based systems with randomized election timeouts (150-300ms); avoids synchronized election cascades across thousands of instances
Datadog metrics pipeline uses idempotent consumers with message deduplication tables; handles duplicate metrics from retried API calls without inflating counts

analogies.analogy: Lamport timestamps are like page numbers in a distributed book: each author (process) increments their counter before writing, ensuring a logical reading order even without synchronized clocks

Vector clocks are like conversation histories: each person (process) remembers what they've heard from everyone else; you can tell if two events are causally connected by checking if one person knows about the other's message
Raft quorum is like jury voting: you need a majority to convict (commit), so even if some jurors disappear (node failures), the verdict stands if >50% remain
Split-brain in Raft is like a kingdom divided: each half thinks it's the legitimate leader, but only the larger half can govern (has quorum); the smaller half is paralyzed
Log replication in Raft is like a ship's captain maintaining a crew logbook: entries are written sequentially, copied to all crew members, and only considered permanent once the majority signs off
Idempotent consumers are like paying a bill with check carbon copies: even if the receipt gets lost and you resend the check, the bank's dedup table prevents double-charging
Byzantine fault tolerance is like a jury with potential liars: Raft assumes honest jurors (CFT, 2f+1 nodes), but BFT assumes some jurors might conspire (3f+1 nodes needed)
Write-ahead logging is like a restaurant hostess writing down orders before giving them to the kitchen: if the system crashes, the order log survives

key_numbers.context: Standard Raft parameter to avoid simultaneous elections
Leader sends heartbeats to followers at this frequency
Follower triggers election if no heartbeat received in this window

key_numbers.metric: Minimum election timeout rule of thumb
key_numbers.context: If RTT is 10ms, election timeout should be ≥100ms to avoid false positives
Cluster at 100 entries/second produces 315 GB/year at 100 bytes/entry
Can tolerate 1 node failure while maintaining quorum

key_numbers.value: 3f+1 nodes required for f byzantine failures
key_numbers.context: Higher requirement than CFT due to potential malicious nodes
Much worse than LAN due to variable packet delay
Uses hardware timestamping at NIC level
Castro & Liskov 1999 algorithm; sub-millisecond latency increase
33 answered Raft questions better than Paxos questions after learning both
Each range replicates independently across fault domains

common_misconceptions.misconception: The network is reliable and messages always arrive in order
common_misconceptions.reality: Fallacy #1: The network can drop packets, reorder messages, and experience arbitrary delays. Raft handles this via sequence numbers, timeouts, and retries
common_misconceptions.consequences: Ignoring network unreliability leads to systems that fail under realistic conditions; split-brain and lost updates are the result

common_misconceptions.misconception: Clock synchronization is good enough; you can use physical timestamps to determine event order
common_misconceptions.reality: Even NTP on LANs has 100+ microsecond skew; clock drift causes forward jumps. Lamport timestamps and vector clocks provide logical ordering without synchronized clocks
common_misconceptions.consequences: Systems using physical timestamps for ordering fail under clock skew, producing stale reads or inconsistent snapshots

common_misconceptions.misconception: Raft and Paxos have equivalent complexity; the choice doesn't matter
common_misconceptions.reality: User studies show 77% of engineers answer Raft questions correctly vs. 23% for Paxos after learning both; Raft's separation of concerns (leader election, log replication, safety) makes implementation easier
common_misconceptions.consequences: Teams choosing Paxos face higher implementation complexity, longer development cycles, and more subtle bugs

common_misconceptions.misconception: Vector clocks tell you which event happened first; they're better than Lamport timestamps
common_misconceptions.reality: Vector clocks detect causality (which events are related), not absolute order. Lamport timestamps impose a total order but lose causal information. Use vector clocks for CRDTs/conflict resolution, Lamport timestamps for consensus/logging
common_misconceptions.consequences: Choosing the wrong clock type wastes O(N) space or loses the ability to detect concurrent updates

common_misconceptions.misconception: Idempotency means retries are safe; I don't need deduplication logic
common_misconceptions.reality: Idempotency prevents state corruption from re-execution, but deduplication (message ID tracking) still prevents spurious reads/reports. Both are needed for exactly-once semantics
common_misconceptions.consequences: Systems report metrics twice, create duplicate charges, or increment counters multiple times despite idempotent handlers

common_misconceptions.misconception: Network partitions are rare; you don't need to design for them
common_misconceptions.reality: Fallacy #6: Network partitions happen regularly in production: switch failures, cable cuts, ISP BGP flaps. Raft is designed to survive partitions gracefully (minority partition becomes read-only)
common_misconceptions.consequences: Systems that ignore partitions either lose data (if they prioritize availability) or suffer extended outages (if they prioritize consistency)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Setting election timeout high (>5 seconds) prevents false-positive elections
common_misconceptions.reality: High timeouts increase unavailability after real leader failure. Sweet spot is broadcastTime * 10 to balance responsiveness and stability; Dynatune (2024) adapts dynamically
common_misconceptions.consequences: Too-high timeouts cause 10+ second recovery; too-low timeouts trigger cascading election storms under load

common_misconceptions.misconception: Snapshot transfer is instant; don't worry about large state machines
common_misconceptions.reality: Snapshot serialization, network transfer, and disk I/O add latency. On busy clusters, lagging followers struggle to install snapshots before the next snapshot arrives
common_misconceptions.consequences: New followers fail to catch up and cluster availability suffers; disk I/O spikes cause performance degradation

common_misconceptions.misconception: Strong consistency is always better than eventual consistency
common_misconceptions.reality: Strong consistency requires coordination (higher latency, lower availability). Eventual consistency trades transient stale data for responsiveness. Choice depends on use case: financial transactions need strong, user feeds can tolerate eventual
common_misconceptions.consequences: Strong consistency everywhere causes cascading failures during network partitions; eventual consistency everywhere causes data loss or user-visible inconsistencies

common_misconceptions.misconception: Quorum consensus prevents all data loss
common_misconceptions.reality: Quorum guarantees consistency under F failures, but losing >F replicas causes permanent data loss. Replication is not backup. You need snapshots + off-cluster backup for durability
common_misconceptions.consequences: Correlated failures (power outage, software bug across cluster) cause total data loss despite quorum replication

common_misconceptions.misconception: Byzantine Fault Tolerance is needed for all distributed systems
common_misconceptions.reality: BFT (3f+1 nodes) is expensive; Crash Fault Tolerance (2f+1 nodes) suffices for most systems (internal networks, trusted operators). Use BFT only if you expect compromised nodes
common_misconceptions.consequences: Over-engineered systems with 50% more nodes and 3x communication overhead for 99% of use cases

common_misconceptions.misconception: Consensus algorithms are purely theoretical; no need to understand the protocol details
common_misconceptions.reality: Understanding leader election, log replication, and safety properties is critical for production tuning, debugging cascading failures, and avoiding split-brain
common_misconceptions.consequences: Operators cannot diagnose etcd hangs, tune timeout parameters, or recover from corruption without protocol knowledge
